subject: Computer Networking
title: PGP and End-to-End Encryption
description: A micro-lesson that makes PGP and End-to-End Encryption usable.

hook.text: TLS encrypts the pipe. PGP encrypts the message itself. Only the intended recipient can decrypt it.

buildup.text: PGP uses a key pair. You encrypt with the recipient's public key. They decrypt with their private key.

discovery.text: No server in the middle can read it. Not Gmail, not your ISP, not the government. True end-to-end.

twist.text: PGP is notoriously hard to use. Key management, trust models, and UX have limited adoption for decades.

climax.text: ProtonMail and Tutanota bake encryption into the UI. Users get PGP-level security without the complexity.

punchline.text: PGP means only you and the recipient can read it. True privacy.

quiz.question: What makes PGP different from TLS for email?
options: PGP encrypts the message itself, not just the connection
